blurb: Intensely clove-scented, fringed pink flowers hover above a silver-blue evergreen mat all spring — a drought-tough, drainage-loving rock-garden gem that positively thrives in the Front Range's lean alkaline soil.
water: Low; drought-tolerant once established; never let roots sit wet
seasons: Bright pink fringed blooms May–Jun; occasional rebloom all summer with deadheading; evergreen blue-gray foliage persists through winter
wildlife: Attracts butterflies and bees; deer- and rabbit-resistant
toxic: Saponins in foliage and stems may cause mild GI upset if ingested; contact dermatitis possible with sensitive skin

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–9 (most cultivars 4–8); completely reliable on the Front Range (~5b–6a). The evergreen mat handles deep cold well provided the soil drains sharply — wet, frozen crowns cause more losses than cold temperatures alone.
care.planting: Set out container plants in spring (Apr–early May) or early fall (Sep) once the worst heat has passed. Plant with the crown exactly at soil level — burying it invites crown rot. In containers, cheddar pinks do well in shallow terracotta or gritty troughs; pot mix must drain sharply (add perlite or fine grit). Container roots are exposed to wider temperature swings — move pots to a sheltered wall over winter or sink them into the ground.
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6 hours of direct sun daily. More sun means sturdier, more compact growth and heavier bloom. Part shade is tolerated but produces looser plants with fewer flowers.
care.soil: Lean, gritty, very well-drained soil is essential — this plant evolved in limestone screes and actually flowers best in poor ground. Boulder's alkaline clay-loam must be amended with coarse grit or decomposed granite to open drainage; raised beds and rock gardens suit it perfectly. Avoid heavy clay and rich, moisture-retentive mixes. Crown rot is the chief killer; do not mulch over the crown.
care.water: Low once established — deep watering once a week or less in summer heat is plenty. Let the soil dry between waterings; wet foliage and soggy roots rot the crown. Water at the base, not overhead. Excellent for xeric borders once the mat has knitted in (usually by the second summer).
care.spacing: Space 12–18 in apart; each mat spreads slowly to 12–18 in wide. In rock gardens or wall crevices, tighter spacing (10–12 in) fills the gap faster.
care.propagation: Easiest by stem cuttings taken in early summer just after the main bloom flush — a 2–3 in non-flowering shoot rooted in gritty mix roots quickly. Division in early spring or after bloom every 3–4 years keeps the mat vigorous as centers die back. Seed is also viable — direct sow or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; barely cover seed, as germination needs light.
care.bloom: May through June on the Front Range (main flush); with prompt deadheading expect intermittent pink flowers throughout summer into early fall. The clove-carnation fragrance is strongest on warm afternoons.
care.feeding: Very light feeding only — an all-purpose liquid fertilizer at half-strength every 6–8 weeks during the growing season is ample. Rich fertilization produces lush, floppy growth and fewer flowers. Skip winter feeding entirely.
care.maintenance: Shear plants lightly by one-third immediately after the main June bloom to encourage a tidy, compact mat and trigger rebloom. Deadhead through summer. Remove any dead, brown patches in spring and trim winter-damaged tips. Divide every 3–4 years when the center dies out. Do not mulch over the crown; leave it exposed for airflow.
care.troubles: Crown rot in wet or poorly drained soil is the main enemy — the fix is sharper drainage, not pesticide. Rust and leaf-spot can appear in humid summers; good airflow and base-only watering prevent them. No serious pests; deer and rabbits leave it alone.
